summary: On September 11, 2001, I witnessed the tragic events at the World Trade Center from Ellis Island. I saw the first tower with flames and thick black smoke rising, and shortly after, the second plane struck the other tower. It was surreal to watch such a familiar part of the New York City skyline engulfed in fire and destruction. The magnitude of the event became clear as we realized this was no accident but a deliberate attack.

I was on Ellis Island when the September 11 attacks happened, and I remember hearing an explosion and then seeing smoke rising from the World Trade Center. The whole day was filled with uncertainty and fear as we watched the second plane hit and realized the scale of the attack. We quickly moved to evacuate the island, prepared medical triage, and awaited further instructions, all while trying to process the enormity of what was unfolding. It was a day that changed everything for all of us.

I was working on Ellis Island on the morning of September 11, 2001, when the attacks on the World Trade Center occurred. From the island, we had a clear view of the towers and witnessed the planes hitting them. Ellis Island quickly became a staging area for evacuation and medical triage as we prepared to receive victims and evacuees from Manhattan. The island, usually quiet and historic, turned into a hub of emergency response and uncertainty that day.

During the September 11 attacks, I saw a plane flying very low over the Statue of Liberty and Ellis Island, and I noticed it had the United Airlines monogram. At first, we thought it might be a firefighting plane coming to help, but then it struck the second tower. Witnessing that moment from Ellis Island was shocking and surreal, as it became clear this was a deliberate attack involving commercial airlines.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, I saw a plane flying low right over the Statue of Liberty before it crashed into the second World Trade Center tower. The Statue of Liberty, a symbol we all know well, was part of the route that plane took, which made the event feel even more personal and close to home. We always knew the statue could be a potential target, but seeing that plane fly over it was chilling.

I was on Ellis Island, just off the southern tip of New York City, when the attacks on the World Trade Center happened. We could see the skyline clearly and watched the events unfold with horror. The city went into lockdown, and many transportation systems like the Staten Island Ferry and subways stopped running, making evacuation and rescue efforts challenging. The whole city was in chaos, and we prepared to assist by receiving evacuees on the island.

After the attacks, we planned to evacuate people from Ellis Island, but it was uncertain if they could get back to Battery Park, which is the usual point of arrival in Manhattan. Battery Park is the southern tip of Manhattan, and with the subway and ferry systems shut down, it was unclear how safe or accessible it would be. We had to coordinate with marine vessels to find safe points for evacuees, and Battery Park was a key location in those plans.

Jersey City was important during the September 11 attacks because it was connected to Ellis Island by a bridge, and we had to be vigilant about security there. We heard reports of suspicious boats near the bridge and had to investigate to ensure no devices were placed to detonate it. Also, the Jersey City Medical Department came over to Ellis Island to help set up triage, showing how the city’s resources supported the emergency response.

Liberty State Park served as a staging area during the September 11 attacks, with ambulances lined up there waiting to assist with the medical response. It was part of the larger emergency network supporting Ellis Island, where we were setting up triage for victims and evacuees. The park’s proximity to the water and to Jersey City made it a strategic location for coordinating rescue efforts.

The Park Police played a crucial role on September 11, 2001, by taking out heavy artillery and forming a game plan to respond to the attacks. They were responsible for security on Ellis Island and helped manage the evacuation and safety of staff and visitors. Their quick response was vital as we dealt with suspicious boats and other potential threats during the chaotic day.

On September 11, the Staten Island Ferry was not operating, which complicated evacuation efforts from Manhattan. With the ferry off and subways shut down, getting people away from Lower Manhattan was a huge challenge. This made Ellis Island an important refuge and staging area for evacuees, as traditional routes like the Staten Island Ferry were unavailable.
